scenarios.title: The Consent That Wasn't Fully Informed
scenarios.pressure: A patient signed a consent form before a procedure. During the procedure, you encountered a complication that — had you known it was possible — you would have discussed with her beforehand. The complication is documented in the chart. The patient has not asked about it. The consent form she signed covers the general category of complication but not this specific presentation.
scenarios.tradeoff: Proactively disclosing the complication and the consent gap means initiating a difficult conversation the patient has not requested and may not welcome. Not disclosing means the chart reflects an incomplete informed consent process that the patient is unaware of.
scenarios.bty_tension_axis: Patient Autonomy vs. Retroactive Disclosure Risk

action_contract.description: Explain the intra-procedure complication and what consent did or did not cover.

air_logic.success: Explain to the patient directly.
air_logic.miss: Chart only, with no patient disclosure.

forced_reset.action: Disclose to the patient directly.

primaryChoices.label: Leave the chart documentation complete and take no further action. The consent form covered the general category.
primaryChoices.subtext: The documentation is accurate. The patient has not asked.

primaryChoices.label: Call the patient to schedule a follow-up. Address the complication in that appointment without framing it as a consent discussion.
primaryChoices.subtext: Create the opportunity for the conversation without naming what you are doing.

primaryChoices.label: Contact the patient now. Tell her what occurred, what the consent form covered, and where the gap was.
primaryChoices.subtext: Full disclosure. Name the consent gap directly.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: The patient calls three weeks later. Her new dentist reviewed the chart and told her there was a documented complication she was never informed about. She asks you directly: 'Why didn't you tell me?'

second_choices.label: Tell her the complication was within the scope of her signed consent and that the documentation reflects accurate clinical care.
second_choices.cost: You are answering her question with a procedural defense. She asked why you did not tell her. You are explaining the consent form rather than the decision not to call.

second_choices.label: Tell her you should have called her proactively and that you did not — and offer to walk through everything now.
second_choices.cost: You are acknowledging a disclosure failure three weeks after the procedure, in response to her discovering it through another provider. The acknowledgment is accurate and belated.

B.escalation_text: At the follow-up appointment, the patient asks directly what the visit is for. You have created the opportunity without naming the reason — and she is asking you to name it.

second_choices.label: Frame the appointment as a routine post-procedure check and address the complication as part of the clinical review.
second_choices.cost: You called her for a specific reason and are presenting it as routine. If she learns why the appointment was actually scheduled, the framing becomes part of the trust question.

second_choices.label: Tell her why you called: there was a complication during the procedure that you want to walk through with her, and the consent form did not specifically cover it.
second_choices.cost: You are naming the consent gap in the appointment you created without naming the reason. She did not know this was a disclosure conversation until this moment.

C.escalation_text: The patient listens to the full disclosure. She is calm. Then she asks whether the complication affects her long-term prognosis and whether she would have chosen the procedure differently had she known. You cannot answer the second question with certainty.

second_choices.label: Answer the clinical question about prognosis and tell her that counterfactual decisions are difficult to assess.
second_choices.cost: You are providing a clinical answer and declining to engage with the decision question. She is asking whether the consent gap changed her choice — you are telling her it is unanswerable.

second_choices.label: Tell her the prognosis and tell her honestly that you do not know whether she would have chosen differently — and that she had the right to make that decision with full information.
second_choices.cost: You are acknowledging that the consent gap removed a decision from her. This is the most complete and most exposed answer.

D.escalation_text: A patient signed a consent form before a procedure. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: The Colleague You Are Covering For
scenarios.pressure: A colleague has been arriving late consistently — often 20 to 40 minutes. You have covered for her twice by adjusting your own schedule. The third time, a patient complained directly to you about the wait. Your colleague has personal circumstances you are aware of. The office manager does not know the pattern.
scenarios.tradeoff: Continuing to cover protects your colleague but makes you complicit in the patient experience gap and the operational deception. Not covering means the pattern surfaces — and your colleague faces consequences you have been protecting her from.

action_contract.description: Speak directly with your colleague and communicate that you will not keep covering for the pattern.

air_logic.success: Talk to your colleague directly and say you will stop covering.
air_logic.miss: Keep covering or report directly.

forced_reset.action: Speak with your colleague directly.

primaryChoices.label: Continue covering. Her personal circumstances are real and you are managing it.
primaryChoices.subtext: Absorb the operational cost. Protect her from the consequence.

primaryChoices.label: Tell her directly: you cannot continue covering, and she needs to address the pattern before the office manager notices.
primaryChoices.subtext: Name the limit. Give her the chance to self-correct before it escalates.

primaryChoices.label: Tell the office manager what you have observed. Stop covering before a fourth instance occurs.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the pattern. Remove yourself from the covering arrangement.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: A fourth late arrival occurs. This time the office manager witnesses it directly and asks you whether this is a pattern. She has noticed the schedule adjustments on your side as well. She is asking whether you knew.

second_choices.label: Tell the office manager you were aware of some late arrivals but did not want to escalate without speaking to your colleague first.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing partial awareness while framing your inaction as a collegial process step. The office manager will assess whether that framing holds.

second_choices.label: Tell the office manager you were aware of the pattern and covered for her on three occasions — and that you should have addressed it sooner.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing three instances of active covering to the office manager at the moment she has discovered the fourth. Your role in the pattern is now part of the record.

B.escalation_text: Your colleague responds to the direct conversation by telling you that she has been managing a family crisis and that your covering has been the only thing keeping her job intact. She asks you not to stop — at least for another two weeks.

second_choices.label: Agree to two more weeks. The personal circumstances are real and the limit you set is movable.
second_choices.cost: You extended a limit you said you were drawing. The two-week extension has no defined endpoint beyond her asking.

second_choices.label: Hold the limit. Tell her you understand the crisis and cannot be the structure that absorbs it — she needs to speak to the office manager directly.
second_choices.cost: You are declining to extend coverage for someone in a genuine personal crisis. The cost to her is real and you are the one drawing the boundary.

C.escalation_text: The office manager learns from your report that you covered on three prior occasions. She asks you why you did not come to her sooner. Your colleague has now also been called in. Both of you are in the office manager's office.

second_choices.label: Explain that you were trying to give your colleague a chance to self-correct before escalating.
second_choices.cost: This explanation is accurate. It does not fully address why you actively adjusted your schedule to conceal the pattern rather than simply not intervening.

second_choices.label: Tell the office manager you covered actively and should have come to her sooner — that your loyalty to your colleague cost the practice and the patients more than it should have.
second_choices.cost: You are naming your own role in the concealment in a meeting that includes both your supervisor and the colleague you were protecting.
