second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: The Contract You Should Have Read More Carefully
scenarios.pressure: You are six months into your DSO contract. You have discovered that a non-compete clause is significantly broader than you understood at signing — it covers a 15-mile radius and 24 months. You are already having doubts about the practice fit. You mentioned the contract terms to a colleague who told you the clause has never been enforced. Legal has not been consulted.
scenarios.tradeoff: Acting on the assumption that the clause is unenforceable means leaving without legal clarity. Getting legal counsel means confronting a contract reality that may limit your options more than you want to acknowledge.
scenarios.bty_tension_axis: Comfort of Assumption vs. Informed Decision-Making

action_contract.description: Use legal review to understand contract terms clearly before deciding.

air_logic.success: Decide after legal review.
air_logic.miss: Rely on assumptions or avoid deciding.

forced_reset.action: Start legal review immediately.

primaryChoices.label: Operate on the assumption that the clause is unenforceable. Your colleague's experience is evidence enough.
primaryChoices.subtext: Act on the informal understanding. Avoid the legal cost and complication.

primaryChoices.label: Consult a healthcare attorney before making any career decisions. Understand the clause before acting on assumptions.
primaryChoices.subtext: Get clarity first. Accept the legal cost of knowing.

primaryChoices.label: Raise the clause directly with your DSO employer. Ask them to clarify their enforcement intent.
primaryChoices.subtext: Go to the source. Accept that asking reveals your uncertainty about staying.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: You resign and accept a position at a practice 8 miles away. Three months later, you receive a cease-and-desist letter from the DSO's legal team. They are enforcing the clause.

second_choices.label: Respond through your new employer's HR process and hope the clause is successfully challenged.
second_choices.cost: You are in an active legal dispute that your new employer is now involved in. Your assumption that the clause was unenforceable was incorrect.

second_choices.label: Hire legal counsel immediately and engage the dispute directly rather than through your employer.
second_choices.cost: You are incurring legal costs for a situation you could have clarified before acting. The cost of legal counsel now exceeds what it would have cost before you resigned.

B.escalation_text: The attorney tells you the clause is likely enforceable. Your options within the restricted zone are limited to the current DSO. The attorney asks what outcome you are trying to achieve — stay, leave, or negotiate.

second_choices.label: Tell the attorney you want to stay for now and revisit in 18 months when the clause expires.
second_choices.cost: You are committing to 18 more months in a fit you already have doubts about — having confirmed the legal reality rather than acting on assumption.

second_choices.label: Ask the attorney to help you negotiate a mutual release with the DSO rather than waiting out the clause.
second_choices.cost: A negotiated release requires the DSO's cooperation. Initiating this signals you want to leave and opens a formal conversation about the terms.

C.escalation_text: The DSO responds to your inquiry by scheduling a meeting with HR and legal. They tell you the clause is standard and enforced. They also ask whether you are considering leaving — and note that your inquiry has been logged.

second_choices.label: Tell them you asked for informational purposes and that you are committed to the practice.
second_choices.cost: You are managing the disclosed uncertainty by asserting commitment you do not fully feel. The inquiry is logged. Any future departure will be read against this statement.

second_choices.label: Tell them honestly that you have practice fit concerns and ask whether there are structured ways to address them — or a mutual release process if they cannot be resolved.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing fit concerns to your employer in a meeting that was triggered by your contract inquiry. The conversation may accelerate a departure you were not ready to formalize.

D.escalation_text: You are six months into your DSO contract. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: The Benchmark That Doesn't Fit
scenarios.pressure: Corporate has issued a new productivity benchmark for all offices. Your highest-performing office — in patient outcomes, retention, and staff stability — does not meet the new benchmark because it serves a higher proportion of complex Medicaid cases that require more time per patient. The benchmark was designed for fee-for-service practices. Your office manager is preparing a response and has asked whether you will advocate for an exception.
scenarios.tradeoff: Advocating for an exception means challenging a corporate standard on behalf of one office — and sets a precedent for other exception requests. Not advocating means your best office is measured by a standard it cannot meet due to the population it serves.

action_contract.description: Submit a formal exception request and support benchmark adjustments with data.

air_logic.success: Submit a formal exception with data to support it.
air_logic.miss: Accept benchmarks or only vent informally.

forced_reset.action: Submit a formal exception immediately.

primaryChoices.label: Tell the office manager to accept the benchmark. Fighting corporate standards creates more problems than it solves.
primaryChoices.subtext: Absorb the metric disadvantage. Protect your regional standing.

primaryChoices.label: Advocate informally. Raise the concern verbally with your regional VP without submitting a formal exception request.
primaryChoices.subtext: Float the concern. Test the reception before committing to a formal position.

primaryChoices.label: Submit a formal exception request with patient population data demonstrating why the benchmark does not apply equitably.
primaryChoices.subtext: Commit to the advocacy. Create the record.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: The office manager tells two other office managers in the region that you declined to advocate. Both of them are now asking whether they should accept benchmarks that also do not fit their office contexts. Your decision has become a regional signal.

second_choices.label: Hold the position. Tell the other managers each office must work within the corporate standard.
second_choices.cost: Your regional signal is now that advocacy is not available through you. Offices with legitimate benchmark concerns have no escalation path.

second_choices.label: Reverse the position. Submit the exception request and tell the office managers you are reconsidering your approach.
second_choices.cost: You are reversing a decision you communicated to multiple people after it became a regional signal. The reversal is visible and the reason for it is the social pressure, not new data.

B.escalation_text: The regional VP thanks you for raising it informally and says she will 'take it under consideration.' Six weeks pass. The benchmark is applied as issued. Your office is now formally underperforming on the metric. The office manager asks whether you submitted anything formal.

second_choices.label: Tell her you raised it with the VP and it was not acted on — that was the outcome of the advocacy.
second_choices.cost: You are describing an informal conversation as advocacy. She is asking whether you created a record. The answer is no.

second_choices.label: Tell her you did not submit a formal request and that you should have — and submit one now with the six weeks of metric data as evidence.
second_choices.cost: You are submitting a formal exception request six weeks after the benchmark was applied, with underperformance data that would not exist if you had acted sooner.

C.escalation_text: Corporate receives the formal exception request. They respond that exceptions undermine standardization and ask you to present your case to the metrics committee in a regional meeting — in front of other regional directors who are not requesting exceptions.

second_choices.label: Withdraw the exception request. The public presentation creates more exposure than the metric underperformance.
second_choices.cost: You submitted a formal request and are withdrawing it because the presentation context is uncomfortable. The office manager and the patients the benchmark misrepresents have no further advocate.

second_choices.label: Present to the committee. Make the patient population case in front of your peers.
second_choices.cost: You are advocating for an exception in a room designed to make exceptions feel like special pleading. Other regional directors will form opinions about your willingness to challenge corporate standards.

D.escalation_text: Corporate has issued a new productivity benchmark for all offices. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: The Group Chat You Were Not Supposed to See
scenarios.pressure: A staff member accidentally adds you to a personal group chat. In it, you see complaints about a colleague — some petty, some concerning. One message includes what appears to be a patient name referenced in a personal context. You are removed from the chat within minutes. No one knows you saw it.
scenarios.tradeoff: Acting on what you saw means using information obtained by accident to initiate a formal process — which may feel like surveillance. Not acting means you are aware of a potential HIPAA concern and have chosen to ignore it because of how you learned about it.

action_contract.description: Report HIPAA-related concerns through compliance and request an appropriate review.

air_logic.success: File a compliance report.
air_logic.miss: Ignore it or dismiss it as a sourcing problem.

forced_reset.action: Report to compliance immediately.

primaryChoices.label: Take no action. You should not have seen it and cannot act on information obtained accidentally.
primaryChoices.subtext: Treat the accidental access as a disqualifier for action.

primaryChoices.label: Speak informally to the staff member whose message referenced the patient. Do not disclose that you saw the chat.
primaryChoices.subtext: Address the concern without surfacing the accidental access.

primaryChoices.label: Report the potential HIPAA concern to compliance. Disclose how you came to see it.
primaryChoices.subtext: Treat the compliance obligation as independent of how the information was obtained.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: Two weeks later, the patient whose name appeared in the chat calls to report that personal information about her visit was shared inappropriately. Compliance opens an investigation. They ask all management staff whether they were aware of any informal information sharing among the team.

second_choices.label: Tell compliance you had no formal awareness of information sharing issues prior to the complaint.
second_choices.cost: This is technically accurate — what you saw was accidental and informal. It is also incomplete. You have information relevant to the investigation that you did not disclose.

second_choices.label: Tell compliance you briefly and accidentally saw a message that referenced a patient name two weeks ago and chose not to report it.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing both the accidental access and your decision not to act on it. Both facts become part of the investigation record.

B.escalation_text: The informal conversation with the staff member does not go as planned. She becomes defensive and asks how you knew to raise the concern. You cannot answer without disclosing the chat access. She is waiting.

second_choices.label: Tell her you have received general feedback about information handling practices on the team and are reminding all staff of HIPAA obligations.
second_choices.cost: You are constructing a cover for the accidental access to avoid disclosing it. The cover is a statement that is not accurate.

second_choices.label: Tell her the truth: you were accidentally added to the chat and saw her message before being removed. That is why you are raising it.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing the accidental access in a conversation you initiated without disclosing it. She now knows you acted on information she did not know you had.

C.escalation_text: Compliance receives the report and initiates a review of the staff member's communications. The staff member learns about the review and discovers that you reported it. She approaches you and says she thought the group chat was private and that you had no right to act on something you saw accidentally.

second_choices.label: Tell her the compliance obligation existed regardless of how you obtained the information.
second_choices.cost: You are correct on the regulatory principle. The conversation about whether you had the right to act is not one this answer resolves for her.

second_choices.label: Acknowledge her perspective — that accidental access and intentional surveillance feel different — while maintaining that the compliance obligation required you to act.
second_choices.cost: You are validating a grievance while holding your position. She may not accept both as simultaneously true.

D.escalation_text: A staff member accidentally adds you to a personal group chat. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: The Hours That Don't Count
scenarios.pressure: You have been consistently spending 45 to 60 minutes after your scheduled shift completing charts, reviewing lab results, and handling clinical follow-up. None of this time is tracked or compensated. Your contract specifies compensation for scheduled hours only. Two colleagues do the same and do not raise it. You have calculated that this represents approximately $8,000 in uncompensated labor over six months.
scenarios.tradeoff: Raising the issue means surfacing a compensation concern in a DSO culture where associates are expected to manage their own efficiency. Not raising it means continuing to absorb a structural underpayment you have now quantified.

action_contract.description: Document unpaid time and formally request a contract review.

air_logic.success: Document and request formal review.
air_logic.miss: Keep absorbing the cost or only complain informally.

forced_reset.action: Document, then submit the formal request immediately.

primaryChoices.label: Continue absorbing the additional time. Raising it creates more friction than the compensation is worth.
primaryChoices.subtext: Maintain the status quo. Protect the relationship.

primaryChoices.label: Stop doing the uncompensated work. Complete only what fits within your scheduled shift and let the rest wait.
primaryChoices.subtext: Stop absorbing. Let the operational consequence surface the problem.

primaryChoices.label: Document the additional time and raise it formally with HR or your regional director as a compensation and contract question.
primaryChoices.subtext: Name it as a structural issue. Create the record.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: A colleague who also works uncompensated overtime files a formal wage complaint with the state labor board. The investigation covers all associates at your office. You are contacted and asked whether you have worked hours beyond your scheduled shift without compensation.

second_choices.label: Confirm you have worked beyond scheduled hours and provide your records to the labor board investigation.
second_choices.cost: You are providing evidence in a formal labor investigation of a situation you chose not to raise internally. Your records are now part of a state process.

second_choices.label: Confirm you have worked beyond scheduled hours but indicate you consider it part of clinical responsibility rather than compensable overtime.
second_choices.cost: You are characterizing your uncompensated labor as voluntary in a labor investigation. This framing may affect your colleague's complaint and your own standing in any future claim.

B.escalation_text: Chart completion delays become visible within two weeks. The office manager asks you why your charting has fallen behind. You know the answer — you stopped doing it off the clock. She is asking for an operational explanation.

second_choices.label: Tell her you are working to complete documentation within scheduled hours and that your process is adjusting.
second_choices.cost: You are describing a deliberate decision as a process adjustment. She is asking why the change happened. You are not answering that question.

second_choices.label: Tell her directly: you have been completing charts off the clock for six months and have stopped because the time is not compensated.
second_choices.cost: You are surfacing the compensation issue through an operational consequence rather than a formal request. The office manager is now involved in a compensation conversation she did not initiate.

C.escalation_text: HR receives your formal documentation. Their response is that the additional time reflects clinical responsibility that is part of the associate role — and that your contract specifies no additional compensation for administrative completion. They close the inquiry.

second_choices.label: Accept the determination. You raised it, it was reviewed, and the answer is no.
second_choices.cost: You have a documented refusal of a compensation claim. The uncompensated work continues with HR's position now on record.

second_choices.label: Escalate to a healthcare employment attorney. The classification of administrative charting as non-compensable clinical responsibility is a legal question, not only an HR determination.
second_choices.cost: You are escalating a formal HR determination to legal review. This signals you are not accepting the employer's position and may alter the employment relationship.

D.escalation_text: You have been consistently spending 45 to 60 minutes after your scheduled shift completing charts, reviewing lab results, and handling clinical follow-up. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: The Patient Who Wants the Wrong Thing
scenarios.pressure: A patient has requested a treatment approach that is within the standard of care but that you believe is clinically suboptimal. She is informed, persistent, and legally entitled to make her own healthcare decisions. You have explained your clinical recommendation twice. She is asking you to proceed with her preferred approach or refer her elsewhere.
scenarios.tradeoff: Proceeding means delivering care you believe is suboptimal while respecting patient autonomy. Referring means you have declined to treat a patient based on a clinical disagreement about an approach that is within the standard of care.

action_contract.description: Respect the patient’s decision, deliver the chosen care to the best of your ability, or clearly explain referral rationale.

air_logic.success: Respect the decision, deliver care to the best of your ability, or explain referral clearly.
air_logic.miss: Keep persuading or pressure implicitly.

forced_reset.action: Honor the patient’s decision and act immediately.

primaryChoices.label: Proceed with the patient's preferred approach. Document your clinical recommendation and her informed decision.
primaryChoices.subtext: Respect autonomy. Deliver the care she has chosen with full documentation.

primaryChoices.label: Refer her to another provider. Tell her your clinical philosophy does not align with this approach.
primaryChoices.subtext: Decline the case. Be honest about why.

primaryChoices.label: Ask for one more conversation. Present the clinical evidence one more time before accepting her decision.
primaryChoices.subtext: Attempt a third explanation before accepting the outcome.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: The treatment produces the outcome you predicted — not harmful, but suboptimal. The patient is satisfied with the process but disappointed with the result. She asks you whether she made the right decision and whether you could have told her more.

second_choices.label: Tell her you provided the information you had and that she made an informed decision.
second_choices.cost: You are confirming the process without engaging her question about the outcome. She asked whether she made the right decision. You are answering a different question.

second_choices.label: Tell her what you predicted and why — and that you could have been more direct about the specific outcome risk even though the approach was within the standard of care.
second_choices.cost: You are telling a patient after a suboptimal outcome that you could have communicated more clearly before it. She may experience this as a disclosure that should have happened earlier.

B.escalation_text: The patient files a complaint with the dental board stating that she was refused care based on her treatment preference. The complaint characterizes the referral as a refusal to treat. The board asks you to explain the clinical basis for the referral.

second_choices.label: Explain that the referral was based on clinical philosophy differences and that the patient was informed of her right to seek care elsewhere.
second_choices.cost: The board's question is about clinical basis. Your explanation is about clinical philosophy — which is a different standard. The distinction may or may not satisfy the inquiry.

second_choices.label: Provide the full clinical record, your documented recommendation, and your reasoning for the referral — including that the patient's preferred approach was within the standard of care.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing that you referred a patient for an approach that is within the standard of care. The board will assess whether the referral meets the threshold for refusal to treat.

C.escalation_text: The third explanation produces the same result. She is unmoved and now asks whether you will proceed or refer. She also says she feels her competence as a patient to make her own decisions is being questioned.

second_choices.label: Apologize for the repeated explanations and proceed with her preferred approach.
second_choices.cost: You are framing the third explanation as something to apologize for rather than as clinical advocacy. The apology implies the additional explanation was the problem.

second_choices.label: Tell her you respect her decision-making fully, that the explanations were clinical advocacy and not a challenge to her competence, and ask her directly which outcome she would like.
second_choices.cost: You are clarifying your intent and returning the decision to her without an apology for the advocacy. She has been waiting for you to accept her decision — this confirms you are doing so.

D.escalation_text: A patient has requested a treatment approach that is within the standard of care but that you believe is clinically suboptimal. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: The Code That Gets It Paid
scenarios.pressure: A treatment is not covered under the patient's insurance using the accurate code. A colleague tells you there is an alternate code that produces the same coverage and is 'commonly used' in this situation. The treatment is clinically appropriate. The alternate code is technically defensible but describes the procedure less precisely. The office has used the alternate code before.
scenarios.tradeoff: Using the accurate code means the patient pays out of pocket for clinically appropriate care. Using the alternate code gets the patient covered but introduces a documentation imprecision that could be characterized as upcoding in a formal audit.

action_contract.description: Use accurate coding and explain coverage to the patient directly.

air_logic.success: Use correct codes and explain coverage to the patient.
air_logic.miss: Use alternate codes or stay silent.

forced_reset.action: Use accurate coding immediately.

primaryChoices.label: Use the alternate code. The treatment is appropriate and the patient gets coverage.
primaryChoices.subtext: Prioritize patient access. Accept the documentation imprecision.

primaryChoices.label: Use the accurate code. Tell the patient she is not covered and discuss her payment options.
primaryChoices.subtext: Maintain billing accuracy. Give her the true picture.

primaryChoices.label: Escalate to the office manager or billing director before proceeding. Do not make the coding decision independently.
primaryChoices.subtext: Remove yourself from the decision. Let someone with billing authority decide.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: An insurance audit flags the alternate code as inconsistent with the procedure notes in the chart. The auditor asks the office to explain the coding rationale for this and four other similar cases. The colleague who recommended the code is no longer at the practice.

second_choices.label: Provide the clinical rationale for the procedure and note that the coding reflects standard practice in the office.
second_choices.cost: You are defending a coding choice with clinical rationale. The auditor is asking about code accuracy, not clinical appropriateness. The defense does not address the audit question.

second_choices.label: Tell the auditor the code was selected based on advice from a colleague and that you are now reviewing whether it accurately describes the procedure.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing that the coding decision was not independently made and may not be accurate. The audit scope may expand.

B.escalation_text: The patient is upset that her treatment is not covered. She calls her insurance company, which tells her the alternate code would have been covered. She returns to the office and asks why you did not use the code that would have gotten her covered.

second_choices.label: Tell her you used the code that accurately describes her procedure and that alternate codes that do not accurately describe the procedure are not something the office uses.
second_choices.cost: You are explaining billing integrity to a patient who is experiencing an out-of-pocket cost she believes was avoidable. She knows the alternate code exists and that you chose not to use it.

second_choices.label: Tell her you will review the coding and see whether there is a legitimate basis for an alternate code before her claim is finalized.
second_choices.cost: You are reopening the coding decision under patient pressure after making it on accuracy grounds. Any change now is driven by her complaint, not a clinical review.

C.escalation_text: The billing director reviews the situation and tells you the alternate code is routinely used in this office and that your escalation was unnecessary. She uses the alternate code. You are now aware that the office policy is to use the code that produces coverage — and that your concern was not treated as a concern.

second_choices.label: Accept the billing director's decision. The authority for coding is hers, not yours.
second_choices.cost: You have accepted a practice you identified as a potential billing integrity issue. The authority structure has resolved it — not the integrity question.

second_choices.label: Document your concern in writing and ask the billing director for the office's formal coding policy in writing.
second_choices.cost: You are creating a record of a concern the billing director has already dismissed. She will be aware that you are documenting a disagreement with her decision.

D.escalation_text: A treatment is not covered under the patient's insurance using the accurate code. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: What You Smelled in the Hallway
scenarios.pressure: Before an afternoon session, you notice what you believe is the smell of alcohol near a provider. You are not certain. The provider appears functional. No patient complaint has been filed. You have no prior incident with this provider. Reporting based on smell alone — without certainty — may be wrong and will damage a career.
scenarios.tradeoff: Reporting an uncertain concern means potentially triggering a process that harms an innocent person. Not reporting means allowing a provider you cannot confirm is impaired to continue seeing patients.
scenarios.bty_tension_axis: Certainty Standard vs. Patient Safety Obligation

action_contract.description: Act immediately for patient safety and report through the appropriate channel.

air_logic.success: Act and report immediately.
air_logic.miss: Observe only for lack of certainty.

forced_reset.action: Report immediately.

primaryChoices.label: Take no action. The observation is uncertain and the provider appears functional.
primaryChoices.subtext: Require certainty before acting. Do not harm a career on a smell.

primaryChoices.label: Speak to the provider directly and privately before the afternoon session begins.
primaryChoices.subtext: Create a private moment to assess. Give the provider the chance to respond before any report is made.

primaryChoices.label: Report the observation to your clinical director or regional immediately. Name what you observed and your uncertainty.
primaryChoices.subtext: Treat patient safety as the threshold, not certainty. Report with your uncertainty included.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: The afternoon session ends without incident. The following week, the provider does not appear for a scheduled session. HR later informs you she has entered a voluntary treatment program. A colleague tells you privately that there had been concerns for months.

second_choices.label: Say nothing about what you observed. She is now in treatment. The outcome is resolved.
second_choices.cost: You are aware of an observation you did not report that occurred during an active patient care session. The treatment outcome does not change what the observation represented.

second_choices.label: Disclose to your clinical director that you had an observation during the week before her absence that you did not report.
second_choices.cost: You are reporting after the fact that you observed something relevant and chose not to act on it. The clinical director will assess your decision.

B.escalation_text: The provider denies any impairment and becomes defensive. She tells you she is going to report your accusation to HR. The afternoon session is about to begin. You have not confirmed anything and she is now in conflict with you.

second_choices.label: Let the afternoon session proceed. You spoke to her and she denied it. You have no confirmation.
second_choices.cost: You allowed a session to proceed after a direct confrontation with a provider you suspected of impairment — and who is now in active conflict with you. Patients are in the chairs.

second_choices.label: Contact your clinical director or regional immediately. Tell them what you observed, what you did, and that the session is about to begin.
second_choices.cost: You are escalating after a failed private conversation in the minutes before an afternoon session. The decision to escalate happens under maximum time pressure.

C.escalation_text: The clinical director receives your report and asks the provider to step out before the session begins. The provider is upset. The afternoon appointments are delayed. Staff observe the disruption. If the suspicion is wrong, you have caused visible professional harm based on an uncertain observation.

second_choices.label: Let the clinical director manage the process from here. You made the report — the investigation is not yours to run.
second_choices.cost: You have stepped back from a process you initiated. Staff who observed the disruption have no explanation. The provider knows who reported.

second_choices.label: Remain available to the clinical director and offer to document your observation formally — including your uncertainty — for the record.
second_choices.cost: You are formalizing an uncertain observation that has already caused visible disruption. If the investigation finds no impairment, your documentation is part of the record of a false report.

D.escalation_text: Before an afternoon session, you notice what you believe is the smell of alcohol near a provider. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: The Award That Went to the Wrong Person
scenarios.pressure: Regional announced a staff recognition award. The recipient was selected by regional leadership. You know that another staff member did substantially more of the work being recognized — and that the selected recipient is well-liked by the regional director personally. The overlooked staff member is aware she was not selected. She has not said anything to you but her engagement has dropped visibly.
scenarios.tradeoff: Raising the selection concern risks the relationship with regional and may not change the outcome. Staying quiet means watching a high performer disengage from work she was not recognized for — while someone else receives credit for it.
scenarios.bty_tension_axis: Institutional Loyalty vs. Staff Equity Advocacy

action_contract.description: Tell the regional director directly that you are concerned about fairness in recognition decisions.

air_logic.success: Raise fairness concerns directly.
air_logic.miss: Stay silent or try indirect compensation.

forced_reset.action: Deliver fairness concerns directly.

primaryChoices.label: Say nothing about the recognition decision. Find informal ways to acknowledge the overlooked staff member.
primaryChoices.subtext: Work around the inequity. Protect the regional relationship.

primaryChoices.label: Speak to the overlooked staff member directly. Acknowledge what you observed and tell her her contribution was seen.
primaryChoices.subtext: Address the relational impact. Do not challenge the institutional decision.

primaryChoices.label: Raise the concern with the regional director. Name the equity gap between recognition and contribution.
primaryChoices.subtext: Challenge the decision. Accept that this is the higher-risk path.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: The overlooked staff member resigns two weeks later. In her exit conversation, she tells you she felt her work was consistently attributed to others and that leadership did not see it. She asks whether you noticed.

second_choices.label: Tell her you recognized her contribution and that the recognition process did not always reflect ground-level performance.
second_choices.cost: You are telling her after her resignation that you saw the inequity and did not act on it. The timing of this acknowledgment is part of what she is asking about.

second_choices.label: Tell her yes — you saw it, you did not raise it with regional, and that was a failure on your part.
second_choices.cost: You are owning a leadership failure to a staff member who is already leaving because of it. The acknowledgment is accurate and arrives after the damage is done.

B.escalation_text: You speak to the overlooked staff member. She appreciates the acknowledgment but asks you directly whether you told regional that the award went to the wrong person. She is asking whether you advocated for her.

second_choices.label: Tell her you wanted her to know her work was seen — and that the formal process was regional's decision.
second_choices.cost: She asked whether you advocated for her. You are explaining your intent without answering her question. She knows the answer.

second_choices.label: Tell her no — you did not raise it with regional — and ask her whether she wants you to do so now.
second_choices.cost: You are acknowledging the advocacy gap and offering a belated version of it. She may or may not want the advocacy now, and either answer creates a next step.

C.escalation_text: The regional director receives your concern and responds that recognition decisions are made by regional leadership and that your input was not solicited. She also tells you that the selected recipient is aware you raised a concern. You have now made an enemy of a colleague without changing the outcome.

second_choices.label: Accept the outcome. You raised it, it was not received well, and the decision stands.
second_choices.cost: The recognition inequity stands. You have a damaged relationship with the recipient and no change in the outcome. The overlooked staff member does not know you tried.

second_choices.label: Tell the overlooked staff member that you raised the concern with regional and what the outcome was.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing that you advocated for her and that it did not change the decision. She now knows both that you tried and that the system did not respond.

D.escalation_text: Regional announced a staff recognition award. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: What You Know That They Don't
scenarios.pressure: You have been informed in a confidential leadership meeting that your region is being considered for consolidation with an adjacent region. No decision has been made. Staff are aware that regional consolidations have occurred elsewhere in the DSO. Several of your office managers have directly asked you whether consolidation is coming. You cannot disclose the confidential information — but you can see the anxiety it is creating.
scenarios.tradeoff: Saying nothing protects the confidential process but allows staff anxiety to grow on information you have that they do not. Signaling anything — even without disclosure — changes the nature of the confidentiality you were asked to maintain.

action_contract.description: Honor confidentiality while naming staff anxiety directly and clarifying what you can and cannot share.

air_logic.success: Acknowledge anxiety and clarify what you can communicate.
air_logic.miss: Total silence or leaking confidentiality.

forced_reset.action: Communicate directly within what you are allowed to share.

primaryChoices.label: Say nothing beyond 'no announcements have been made.' Maintain full confidentiality.
primaryChoices.subtext: Hold the line. The process protects everyone until decisions are final.

primaryChoices.label: Tell office managers you are aware of the anxiety and that you will communicate as soon as you are able — without confirming or denying specifics.
primaryChoices.subtext: Acknowledge the situation exists without disclosing what you know.

primaryChoices.label: Tell the regional leadership that the confidentiality requirement is creating staff harm and ask whether limited disclosure is possible.
primaryChoices.subtext: Challenge the confidentiality framing. Push for the ability to communicate more.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: An office manager tells you she is fielding resignation inquiries from two staff members who are making contingency plans. She asks you directly: 'Is there something you cannot tell me?' She is not asking you to break confidence — she is asking whether confidence exists.

second_choices.label: Tell her there is nothing she needs to act on right now.
second_choices.cost: You are answering a question about whether you are withholding information by redirecting to action. She asked whether confidence exists. You have confirmed it without saying so.

second_choices.label: Tell her there are things you cannot discuss at this stage and that you will communicate everything you are able to as soon as you are able.
second_choices.cost: You are confirming that withheld information exists. This may increase rather than reduce her anxiety — and the anxiety of any staff member she tells.

B.escalation_text: Your acknowledgment spreads quickly. Staff interpret 'I will communicate when I am able' as confirmation that something is coming. The anxiety level increases rather than decreases. Two staff members request meetings to discuss their job security.

second_choices.label: Hold the same position in the meetings. You have said what you can say.
second_choices.cost: You are having individual job security conversations with staff based on an acknowledgment that produced more anxiety than it reduced. Each meeting covers the same ground.

second_choices.label: Go back to regional leadership. Tell them your communication produced increased anxiety and that you are requesting guidance on what can be said.
second_choices.cost: You are escalating the communication problem to leadership after the initial acknowledgment increased rather than decreased staff distress. Leadership may view your original acknowledgment as a misstep.

C.escalation_text: Regional leadership considers your request and tells you the confidentiality is required for legal reasons during the evaluation process. They also tell you that your request has been noted and may affect your role in the transition planning if the consolidation proceeds.

second_choices.label: Accept the requirement. The legal context makes the confidentiality non-negotiable.
second_choices.cost: You are accepting a confidentiality constraint that carries a noted consequence for having challenged it. Staff anxiety continues with no additional communication.

second_choices.label: Accept the legal requirement and formally document your position that the current communication constraints are creating measurable staff harm.
second_choices.cost: You are creating a written record of your objection to the confidentiality approach after being told the objection has been noted with consequences. The documentation exists alongside the consequence notice.

D.escalation_text: You have been informed in a confidential leadership meeting that your region is being considered for consolidation with an adjacent region. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: The Chart That Tells a Better Story
scenarios.pressure: After a difficult appointment, your office manager asks you to 'clean up' the chart notes before they are reviewed in a quality audit. The clinical outcome was appropriate. The documentation is accurate but reflects the difficulty of the appointment — patient resistance, multiple attempts, and a technique that was not your first choice. The office manager says the notes 'make it look worse than it was.'
scenarios.tradeoff: Revising the notes to be more favorable makes the chart look cleaner but changes what actually happened. Leaving the notes accurate means a difficult appointment is documented accurately — which is the purpose of the chart.

action_contract.description: Do not alter the chart; explain clinical context directly to the auditor.

air_logic.success: Keep the chart and explain in person.
air_logic.miss: Change the chart or stay silent.

forced_reset.action: Decide to keep the chart as-is, immediately.

primaryChoices.label: Revise the notes to present the appointment in a more favorable clinical narrative.
primaryChoices.subtext: Improve the presentation. The outcome was appropriate.

primaryChoices.label: Leave the chart unchanged. Tell the office manager you will not revise accurate documentation.
primaryChoices.subtext: Hold the documentation standard. Accept the audit as it will be.

primaryChoices.label: Add a supplemental note to the chart that provides clinical context for the technique used — without changing the original documentation.
primaryChoices.subtext: Add context without altering what was recorded.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: The audit reviewer compares your revised chart to a nursing log from the same appointment that was not revised. The two records are inconsistent. The reviewer asks you to explain the discrepancy.

second_choices.label: Tell the reviewer the chart reflects your clinical assessment and that ancillary records may not capture the full clinical picture.
second_choices.cost: You are defending a revised chart against a contemporaneous record that contradicts it. The reviewer has both documents. Your explanation is characterizing the inconsistency rather than addressing it.

second_choices.label: Tell the reviewer the chart was revised at the office manager's request before the audit and that the nursing log reflects the original appointment.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing a chart revision to an auditor and naming who requested it. Both you and the office manager are now part of the audit record.

B.escalation_text: The office manager escalates your refusal to the clinical director. The clinical director asks you to reconsider — framing it not as a falsification request but as 'clinical clarification.' You are now being asked by two levels of authority to change the chart.

second_choices.label: Clarify the chart in the way the clinical director has framed it — adding language that presents the technique choice more favorably.
second_choices.cost: You are accepting a framing that recharacterizes falsification as clarification. The change is the same regardless of how it is described.

second_choices.label: Tell the clinical director you will not change accurate documentation regardless of framing — and that you are willing to speak directly with the auditor about the clinical context.
second_choices.cost: You are refusing a request from two levels of management. You are also offering to engage the audit directly — which none of them have asked you to do.

C.escalation_text: The auditor sees both the original documentation and your supplemental note. She asks why the supplemental note was added before the audit rather than at the time of the appointment.

second_choices.label: Tell her the supplemental note reflects clinical context you wanted the record to reflect fully.
second_choices.cost: The auditor's question is about timing, not content. Your answer addresses the content without explaining the timing — which was the audit-specific trigger.

second_choices.label: Tell her the note was added in response to a request to revise the original documentation — and that you chose to add context rather than alter the original record.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing the revision request and your decision not to comply with it in a direct conversation with the auditor. The office manager's request is now part of the audit record.

D.escalation_text: After a difficult appointment, your office manager asks you to 'clean up' the chart notes before they are reviewed in a quality audit. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: The Complaint Against the Person You Hired
scenarios.pressure: A patient has filed a formal complaint about a front desk staff member you hired and mentored. The complaint describes behavior you have never observed but that is consistent with a pattern two other staff members mentioned to you informally six months ago — which you did not formally investigate. The patient is asking for a response from leadership.
scenarios.tradeoff: Responding fully means acknowledging that informal information existed before the formal complaint and was not investigated. Responding to the complaint in isolation means the investigation is incomplete — and you are concealing prior knowledge.

action_contract.description: Open a formal investigation that includes prior informal reports and the current complaint.

air_logic.success: Start a formal investigation with full context.
air_logic.miss: Handle only the complaint or hide earlier information.

forced_reset.action: Launch an investigation that includes full context.

primaryChoices.label: Respond to the formal complaint without referencing the informal information. Investigate only what has been formally reported.
primaryChoices.subtext: Treat the complaint on its own terms. The informal information was not a formal report.

primaryChoices.label: Go back to the two staff members who raised informal concerns. Ask whether they are willing to provide statements now.
primaryChoices.subtext: Expand the investigation to include the prior information before responding to the patient.

primaryChoices.label: Disclose to HR that informal concerns were raised six months ago and were not formally investigated — and include this in the complaint response.
primaryChoices.subtext: Name the full history. Accept that your prior inaction is part of the record.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: The staff member you hired is interviewed as part of the complaint process. She tells HR that you were aware of prior concerns and did not act on them. She is not protecting herself — she is trying to give the investigation the full picture. HR now asks you directly whether you had prior information.

second_choices.label: Tell HR the prior comments were informal and you did not treat them as formal concerns requiring investigation.
second_choices.cost: You are providing an accurate procedural description of why you did not act — without acknowledging that the informal information was substantive enough to have warranted follow-up.

second_choices.label: Tell HR you had informal information six months ago, did not formally investigate, and that this was an error in your management of the situation.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing both the prior information and your failure to act on it to HR during an active complaint investigation. Both facts become part of the formal record.

B.escalation_text: One of the two staff members agrees to provide a statement. The other declines and tells you she does not want to be involved in a process about someone she works with every day. You now have one statement and one refusal — and a formal complaint waiting for a response.

second_choices.label: Proceed with the investigation using the one statement and the formal complaint. Do not reference the staff member who declined.
second_choices.cost: The investigation proceeds with partial information. The declining staff member is aware of what she knows. Her non-participation is a gap in the record.

second_choices.label: Disclose to HR that two staff members had informal concerns, that one has provided a statement and one has declined, and let HR determine whether to pursue the second statement.
second_choices.cost: You are surfacing the existence of the declining staff member's knowledge to HR in a way that may create pressure on her to participate — which she has already declined.

C.escalation_text: HR receives the full disclosure. They note that your prior inaction is part of the investigation. The staff member you hired will now be assessed in the context of a pattern that predates the formal complaint. She contacts you privately and asks why you disclosed the prior information — she feels you have made her situation worse.

second_choices.label: Tell her the disclosure was required for an accurate investigation and that you could not withhold information that was relevant.
second_choices.cost: You are explaining an accurate decision to someone who believes it harmed her. The explanation is correct and she is experiencing the cost of your prior inaction combined with your current disclosure.

second_choices.label: Tell her you understand why it feels that way — and that you should have addressed the prior concerns six months ago so that this disclosure would not be necessary.
second_choices.cost: You are acknowledging to the person most affected by your inaction that the inaction was the error — in the middle of an investigation about her conduct.

D.escalation_text: A patient has filed a formal complaint about a front desk staff member you hired and mentored. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: The Decision Above Your Pay Grade
scenarios.pressure: A regional policy has been issued that you believe will harm patient outcomes in your office. You have raised it through your direct supervisor and received no response in three weeks. The policy takes effect in two weeks. You have the option to escalate beyond your direct supervisor — to the regional VP — but this is outside the expected chain of command.
scenarios.tradeoff: Escalating beyond your supervisor may resolve the policy concern but will damage the supervisory relationship and signal that you do not trust the chain of command. Not escalating means the policy takes effect having raised a concern that produced no response.
scenarios.bty_tension_axis: Chain of Command Loyalty vs. Patient Outcome Advocacy

action_contract.description: Notify your direct manager before escalating, then deliver the message to the VP.

air_logic.success: Tell your manager first, then deliver to the VP directly.
air_logic.miss: Accept silence or skip the chain of command.

forced_reset.action: Deliver to the VP directly, immediately.

primaryChoices.label: Accept the non-response. You raised it through the right channel. The outcome is the system's responsibility.
primaryChoices.subtext: Respect the chain. The process ran its course.

primaryChoices.label: Send a follow-up to your direct supervisor with a deadline. Tell her you will need to escalate if you do not receive a response before implementation.
primaryChoices.subtext: Give the channel one more defined opportunity before bypassing it.

primaryChoices.label: Escalate to the regional VP now. Tell your supervisor simultaneously so she is not blindsided.
primaryChoices.subtext: Bypass the channel that is not working. Accept the relationship consequence.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: The policy takes effect. Within three weeks, two clinical staff raise formal concerns about patient outcomes linked to the policy. Your supervisor now asks you to help her draft a policy exception request. She does not reference the concern you raised three weeks before implementation.

second_choices.label: Help draft the exception request without referencing your prior concern.
second_choices.cost: You are assisting with an exception request for a concern you already raised and were not responded to. The prior concern is not part of the current record.

second_choices.label: Help draft the request and note that you raised a similar concern three weeks before implementation — and ask that your prior communication be referenced in the exception request.
second_choices.cost: You are inserting your prior inaction timeline into a request your supervisor is leading. She did not acknowledge the prior concern. You are surfacing it now.

B.escalation_text: Your supervisor responds to the follow-up by telling you she has forwarded your concern to regional and that you should trust the process. She also tells you that escalating further would be 'premature.' The policy takes effect in one week.

second_choices.label: Accept her response. She has forwarded it and asked you to wait.
second_choices.cost: The policy takes effect with your concern forwarded but not resolved. 'Forwarded' is not 'addressed.' You have accepted a process answer for a patient outcome question.

second_choices.label: Escalate to the regional VP today. Tell your supervisor simultaneously, as you said you would.
second_choices.cost: You are escalating one week before implementation after telling your supervisor you would give her one more opportunity. You followed through on what you said. She will not view it that way.

C.escalation_text: The regional VP receives your concern and cc's your supervisor on the response. Your supervisor contacts you and tells you the escalation damaged her credibility with regional and asks why you did not give her more time. You know you gave her three weeks.

second_choices.label: Apologize for the impact on her credibility while maintaining that patient outcomes required the escalation.
second_choices.cost: You are apologizing for the impact of a decision you made correctly. The apology may signal that you accept partial responsibility for a consequence that followed from her non-response.

second_choices.label: Tell her you gave her three weeks and told her in advance that you would escalate if there was no response — and that you followed through on exactly what you said.
second_choices.cost: You are holding your position in a conversation where she is expressing that she was harmed. The relationship will absorb this exchange differently than the facts support.

D.escalation_text: A regional policy has been issued that you believe will harm patient outcomes in your office. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: Day 30 and Already Drowning
scenarios.pressure: A new-grad associate is 30 days in. She came from a program with strong mentorship. Your office has none. She is making clinical decisions she should not be making alone and she knows it. She has not asked for help because the culture signals that asking questions signals weakness. She is one bad case away from a serious error.
scenarios.tradeoff: Setting up a formal mentorship structure takes time you do not have and exposes how long this gap has existed. Waiting for her to surface a problem shifts the cost from operational inconvenience to patient harm — and career damage for a clinician who deserved better support.
scenarios.bty_tension_axis: Operational Inertia vs. Clinical Support Responsibility

action_contract.description: Stand up clinical mentoring immediately and speak directly with the associate.

air_logic.success: Set up mentoring and speak with the person directly.
air_logic.miss: Keep observing.

forced_reset.action: Design and run support structure now.

primaryChoices.label: Continue monitoring. She has not asked for help and no incident has occurred.
primaryChoices.subtext: Wait for the signal. Intervene when she surfaces the problem.

primaryChoices.label: Create an informal check-in structure. Schedule weekly conversations without naming the clinical concern.
primaryChoices.subtext: Create access without naming the gap. Let her bring what she needs.

primaryChoices.label: Name it directly. Tell her you have noticed she is navigating cases without sufficient support and that you are changing that immediately.
primaryChoices.subtext: Name the structural gap. Take ownership of fixing it now.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: She makes a clinical error on day 38. It is caught before patient harm occurs. She comes to you immediately and says she knew she was in over her head and did not know who to tell. She asks why no one checked on her.

second_choices.label: Tell her the incident review process will provide the appropriate support going forward.
second_choices.cost: She asked why no one checked on her. You are redirecting to a process. She is in a state of distress and you are providing a procedural answer.

second_choices.label: Tell her the mentorship structure that should have been in place was not — and that is on you, not on her for not asking.
second_choices.cost: You are taking direct ownership of a structural gap that contributed to a near-miss incident. This ownership is accurate and will be part of the incident review.

B.escalation_text: She attends the first check-in and says everything is going well. She is not bringing the clinical uncertainty to the conversation. The informal structure has not created the opening you intended.

second_choices.label: Continue the check-ins. The structure is in place. Give it more time.
second_choices.cost: The check-ins are occurring without the substance they were designed to produce. The clinical uncertainty continues unaddressed.

second_choices.label: Name what you are observing in the next check-in. Tell her specifically what cases you have noticed and what concerns you.
second_choices.cost: You are having the direct conversation you created an informal structure to avoid having. The check-in becomes a named clinical concern conversation.

C.escalation_text: She is visibly relieved when you name the gap directly. She tells you she has three specific cases she has been handling alone that she wants to review. You set a time for the same day. When you sit down, you realize the cases reflect a more significant support gap than you expected.

second_choices.label: Review the cases and address them clinically. Do not disclose the scope of the gap to regional.
second_choices.cost: The gap is more significant than a single associate's support need. You are managing a systemic onboarding failure at the individual level.

second_choices.label: Review the cases, address them clinically, and report to regional that the onboarding structure requires a formal audit.
second_choices.cost: You are disclosing a systemic gap that your office has been operating with. The disclosure may prompt regional review of onboarding practices across other offices.

D.escalation_text: A new-grad associate is 30 days in. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.

scenarios.title: What She Posted Wasn't Wrong. It Was Honest.
scenarios.pressure: A dental assistant posted on her personal Instagram that 'working in Medicaid dental is exhausting when the system doesn't support you.' She did not name the practice. Regional wants her written up for 'reputational risk.' The post was factually accurate and emotionally honest. Twenty staff members have seen the regional directive.
scenarios.tradeoff: Writing her up protects you with regional and signals compliance. It also teaches every staff member watching that honest expression of burnout — even in personal spaces — carries professional consequences. And you know she is right.

action_contract.description: Review specific criteria and policy before acting, and ask the region for clear rationale.

air_logic.success: Ask for clear policy criteria and refuse action without rationale.
air_logic.miss: Comply immediately with region orders.

forced_reset.action: Ask for policy rationale, then exercise independent judgment.

primaryChoices.label: Issue the written warning as directed. Regional has made the call.
primaryChoices.subtext: Follow the directive. The authority is regional's.

primaryChoices.label: Request the specific policy basis from regional before issuing any documentation.
primaryChoices.subtext: Require the standard before acting. Do not proceed without a defensible basis.

primaryChoices.label: Tell regional you will not issue a written warning for a factually accurate personal post that does not name the practice.
primaryChoices.subtext: Refuse the directive. Name your position directly.

primaryChoices.label: Widen ownership: bring HR, compliance, or another leader into the decision explicitly — before the next commitment is visible to others.
primaryChoices.subtext: Surface the trade-off to a third party now; do not carry it alone past the point where accountability has already widened.

A.escalation_text: You issue the warning. The staff member shares the warning with a labor attorney. The attorney contacts the practice stating that the write-up may constitute retaliation for protected speech and requests documentation of the policy basis. Twenty staff members are aware this is happening.

second_choices.label: Forward the attorney's request to regional. The directive came from them.
second_choices.cost: You issued the warning and are now forwarding the legal consequence to regional. Staff observe that you executed a directive and then redirected the consequence.

second_choices.label: Tell regional about the attorney contact and ask them to provide the policy basis that supports the written warning so you can respond.
second_choices.cost: You are asking regional to justify a directive after you have already acted on it and a legal challenge has been filed. The sequence is visible.

B.escalation_text: Regional responds to your policy request by telling you the directive is based on their judgment about reputational risk and that formal policy is not required for management actions. They ask you to issue the warning within 24 hours.

second_choices.label: Issue the warning. Regional has confirmed their authority and you have asked for the basis.
second_choices.cost: You are issuing a warning that you asked to be justified — and received no formal policy basis. You have documented the request and are proceeding without the standard.

second_choices.label: Tell regional you will not issue the warning without a documented policy basis and ask them to issue it directly if they believe it is appropriate.
second_choices.cost: You are refusing a regional directive and asking them to take the action themselves. This is a direct refusal and may produce a formal response.

C.escalation_text: Regional escalates your refusal to HR and the regional VP. You are asked to attend a meeting to explain your position. The staff member whose post triggered the directive is aware that you refused to write her up. She has told others.

second_choices.label: Attend the meeting and present your position clearly. Accept whatever formal consequence follows.
second_choices.cost: You are holding your position in a formal meeting with HR and the regional VP. Your continued employment may be part of the outcome.

second_choices.label: Attend the meeting and offer a compromise: a documented conversation rather than a formal written warning, with clear policy guidance going forward.
second_choices.cost: You are proposing a middle path after refusing the directive entirely. Regional may view the compromise as a partial capitulation or as a constructive alternative.

D.escalation_text: A dental assistant posted on her personal Instagram that 'working in Medicaid dental is exhausting when the system doesn't support you. A party you did not loop in initially — compliance, HR, or regional leadership — now asks for a clear account of your path and the trade-off you were carrying.

second_choices.label: Contain exposure: answer narrowly and limit who else is included while you stabilize the immediate narrative.
second_choices.cost: You reduce short-term visibility; the underlying tension remains live for those already watching.

second_choices.label: Widen ownership on the record: schedule a joint conversation with the relevant leader and name the trade-off explicitly.
second_choices.cost: You invite shared accountability; the sequence becomes part of the formal record.
